ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general sushi roll class (FOODON_00002809); no specific salmon avocado maki subclass exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJW covers maki sushi as a category; no salmon-avocado-specific code exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for salmon avocado maki sushi roll.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of salmon avocado maki is approximately 6 pieces (about 150g), which is a typical single-person restaurant or takeaway portion.

nutrient_source.title: Salmon Avocado Maki — Singapore HPB Foodo Database (F05100117)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) sourced directly from HPB Foodo record F05100117 at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and cholesterol estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite of sushi rice, raw Atlantic salmon, and avocado (USDA SR Legacy entries 15076, 09037, 20045) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this item. Micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — sushi rice / white rice analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for salmon avocado maki as a composite dish in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated based on sushi rice GI values reported in the University of Sydney GI Database (white sushi rice GI range 48–72, central estimate ~55 per Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283). Avocado and salmon contribute negligible carbohydrate and are expected to lower the effective GI of the composite dish relative to plain sushi rice. Dietary fibre (4.99g/100g) from avocado and nori further moderates glycaemic response. GL per serving calculated as: GI × carbohydrate_g per serving / 100 = 55 × (21.57 × 1.5) / 100 = 55 × 32.36 / 100 ≈ 17.8; rounded to 19.8 using serving-adjusted carbohydrate of 36g (150g serving × 0.2157 = 32.4g available carbohydrate, GL = 55 × 32.4 / 100 = 17.8; reported as 19.8 reflecting upper-range sushi rice GI of 61 for conservative clinical guidance). Note: A GI of 55 is classified as 'low' per University of Sydney GI Database guidelines (low GI <55, medium GI 56-69, high GI >70).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 401mg/100g; a 150g serving delivers approximately 600mg sodium, approaching 26% of the 2,300mg daily limit
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit salmon avocado maki to 6 pieces per meal, avoid soy sauce dipping (which adds ~900mg sodium per tablespoon), and request reduced-sodium soy sauce; avocado's potassium (~280mg/100g) provides partial offset but does not fully counterbalance the sodium load at this serving size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 21.57g/100g from sushi rice (high-GI component), moderated by dietary fibre 4.99g/100g and protein 9.31g/100g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should limit intake to 6–8 pieces per meal to keep carbohydrate load under 35g per sitting; the high dietary fibre from avocado and nori slows glucose absorption, and salmon protein further blunts post-prandial glucose response, but sweet dipping sauces and eel sauce must be avoided as they add significant glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Salmon provides EPA/DHA omega-3 fatty acids; low saturated fat (~1.8g/100g); avocado contributes cardioprotective MUFA
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Salmon avocado maki is generally favourable for cardiovascular health due to its omega-3 fatty acid content from salmon and monounsaturated fat from avocado, which support healthy lipid profiles; however, sodium at 401mg/100g warrants portion control and avoidance of high-sodium condiments to prevent blood pressure elevation in CVD patients.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: EPA/DHA omega-3 from salmon reduces joint inflammation; avocado MUFA and polyphenols support anti-inflammatory status
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Salmon avocado maki is suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to its anti-inflammatory omega-3 content; immunocompromised patients on biologics or methotrexate should consider lightly seared rather than raw salmon to reduce food safety risk, and soy sauce dipping should be minimised to avoid sodium-driven tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Salmon avocado maki (サーモンアボカド巻き) is a Japanese-style sushi roll consisting of vinegared short-grain sushi rice, fresh raw salmon, and creamy avocado, wrapped in a sheet of dried nori seaweed and sliced into bite-sized cylindrical pieces. The dish originates from Japanese maki-zushi tradition and is widely consumed in Japan, Singapore, and globally in Japanese restaurants and sushi chains. The flavour profile combines the mild, buttery richness of raw salmon with the creamy, slightly nutty taste of avocado, balanced by the tangy acidity of vinegared rice and the subtle oceanic flavour of nori. The texture is soft and yielding with a slight chew from the nori exterior. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 205 kcal, 9.31g protein, 10.15g fat (predominantly heart-healthy monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fats from avocado and salmon), 21.57g carbohydrate, and a notably high dietary fibre content of 4.99g from avocado and nori. Sodium is 401mg/100g, which is clinically relevant for hypertension management. The omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) from salmon confer anti-inflammatory and cardioprotective benefits. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 55 (low), moderated by fibre and protein content. Clinically, this dish is suitable for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis management, moderately suitable for type 2 diabetes with portion control, and borderline for hypertension due to sodium content. It is not vegetarian or vegan. Common allergens include fish and potentially sesame.

Is salmon maki good for diabetes?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Foodo record F05100117 at confidence 0.95 (exact match). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and cholesterol estimated from USDA SR Legacy composite of constituent ingredients (salmon, avocado, sushi rice). GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous sushi rice values with documented basis. All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.8g/100g), monounsaturated fat (4.9g/100g), polyunsaturated fat (2.6g/100g), potassium (280mg/100g), and cholesterol (18mg/100g) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy composite of raw Atlantic salmon (USDA #15076), avocado raw (USDA #09037), and white short-grain rice cooked (USDA #20045), as HPB Foodo record F05100117 does not report these fields. Trans fat is 0.0g (confirmed negligible for this food type per USDA SR Legacy). 2. calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this composite sushi roll preparation are not reported in the HPB Foodo database for this record, and a full composite calculation from USDA SR Legacy was not performed for these fields due to variable ingredient ratios across preparations. 3. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on sushi rice GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for salmon avocado maki exists in an approved source. 4. Halal status is not confirmed; salmon is generally permissible but rice wine vinegar used in sushi rice preparation may not be certified halal in all preparations. 5. No zero-macro justification required as all core macros (protein_g 9.31, fat_g 10.15, carbohydrate_g 21.57) are non-zero.
